title: Ten words of her language changed the price
summary: Narrate how learning a handful of phrases in a market seller's own language turned a daily transaction into a real relationship across languages and cultures.
topic: How can learning a few words of someone else's language change a relationship?
prompt: Write a 3-paragraph story (400–500 words) about a foreigner who learns a few phrases in a market seller's language and watches an ordinary transaction slowly become a friendship. Tell it as a narrative with concrete moments, not as a list of tips. Use the past simple and present perfect, at least one conditional, and 'although' or 'however' at least once.
hintStarters: For the first month I pointed at the vegetables and held out my money, and neither of us said a word beyond…
Although my pronunciation was terrible and I mixed up the tones almost every time, the moment I tried…
If I had stayed silent and simply kept paying, I would never have learned that…

hintVocab.phrase: to greet someone in their language
hintVocab.meaningVi: chào ai đó bằng ngôn ngữ của họ
phá vỡ sự ngượng ngùng ban đầu
cảm thấy như người ngoài cuộc

hintVocab.phrase: to set aside the best produce
hintVocab.meaningVi: để dành sản phẩm ngon nhất
bắc cầu qua một khoảng cách

sampleText:
For my first month in the city, I bought my vegetables in silence. Every morning I stopped at the same stall, pointed at the tomatoes, held out a note, and took my change with a nod. The woman who ran the stall — I did not yet know her name was Tien — gave me the same flat, busy smile she gave everyone. I was a customer, nothing more. I told myself the language was simply too hard, and I had a good excuse: I was only there for a year, and the tones defeated me every time I tried.

Then one evening I sat down and learned ten phrases. Not grammar, not verbs — just ten things a person might say at a market. Good morning. How much is this? It looks very fresh. Thank you, see you tomorrow. The next day I said the first one, badly, and Tien stopped what she was doing and laughed — not unkindly, but with genuine surprise, the way you laugh when a dog does something almost human. She made me say it again. She corrected the tone. A man at the next stall leaned over to listen. By the end of that exchange I had spent the same money as always, but something had changed: I had stopped being a wallet that appeared each morning and become a person who was, however clumsily, trying. Although I still made mistakes, she now waited for me to attempt the words before she answered.

The friendship grew from there, one phrase at a time. Tien began setting aside the best herbs for me before the morning rush. She taught me the words for things I pointed at, and she has corrected my pronunciation so patiently and so often that I now dream in her tones. One day she told me, slowly, so I would understand, that her son had moved abroad and that almost no foreigner had ever bothered to learn a single word of her language. If I had stayed silent that whole year, I would have left the city thinking it was a cold place full of busy strangers. Instead I learned that the coldness had been mine — a wall I had built out of my own fear of sounding foolish. The ten phrases did not make me fluent. They were never about fluency. They were a way of saying, in a language I barely knew, that she was worth the effort of trying. And that, it turned out, was the whole message.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to mark an earlier change inside a past story
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had stopped being a wallet that appeared each morning and become a person who was… trying' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành (had + V3) để chỉ một sự thay đổi đã hoàn tất TRƯỚC một thời điểm khác trong quá khứ ('by the end of that exchange'). Nó giúp sắp xếp thứ tự các sự kiện trong câu chuyện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had stopped being a wallet that appeared each morning and become a person who was… trying' uses the past perfect (had + past participle) to mark a change already complete BEFORE another past point ('by the end of that exchange'). It orders the events within the story.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional for the road not taken
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I had stayed silent that whole year, I would have left the city thinking it was a cold place' dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 (if + past perfect, would have + V3) để tưởng tượng một quá khứ KHÔNG xảy ra — điều người kể đã suýt làm nhưng cuối cùng đã không làm.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I had stayed silent that whole year, I would have left the city thinking it was a cold place' uses the third conditional (if + past perfect, would have + past participle) to imagine a past that did NOT happen — the road the narrator nearly took but ultimately did not.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong tháng đầu tiên ở thành phố, tôi mua rau trong im lặng. Mỗi sáng tôi dừng lại ở cùng một quầy hàng, chỉ vào cà chua, chìa ra một tờ tiền, và nhận tiền thối với một cái gật đầu. Người phụ nữ trông coi quầy hàng — lúc đó tôi chưa biết bà tên là Tiên — trao cho tôi cùng một nụ cười nhạt nhẽo, bận rộn mà bà trao cho mọi người. Tôi là một khách hàng, không hơn. Tôi tự nhủ rằng ngôn ngữ này đơn giản là quá khó, và tôi có một cái cớ chính đáng: tôi chỉ ở đó một năm, và các thanh điệu đánh bại tôi mỗi lần tôi thử.

Rồi một buổi tối tôi ngồi xuống và học mười câu. Không phải ngữ pháp, không phải động từ — chỉ là mười điều mà một người có thể nói ở chợ. Chào buổi sáng. Cái này bao nhiêu tiền? Trông tươi quá. Cảm ơn, hẹn gặp ngày mai. Ngày hôm sau tôi nói câu đầu tiên, một cách tệ hại, và Tiên dừng việc đang làm và bật cười — không phải cười nhạo, mà với sự ngạc nhiên thực sự, kiểu bạn cười khi một con chó làm điều gì đó gần như của con người. Bà bắt tôi nói lại lần nữa. Bà sửa thanh điệu. Một người đàn ông ở quầy bên cạnh nghiêng người sang để lắng nghe. Đến cuối lần trao đổi đó tôi đã tiêu cùng số tiền như mọi khi, nhưng một thứ đã thay đổi: tôi đã thôi là một cái ví xuất hiện mỗi sáng và trở thành một con người đang, dù vụng về, cố gắng. Mặc dù tôi vẫn mắc lỗi, giờ đây bà chờ tôi cố gắng nói các từ trước khi bà trả lời.

Tình bạn lớn lên từ đó, từng câu một. Tiên bắt đầu để dành những loại rau thơm ngon nhất cho tôi trước giờ cao điểm buổi sáng. Bà dạy tôi từ chỉ những thứ tôi chỉ vào, và bà đã sửa cách phát âm của tôi kiên nhẫn đến vậy và thường xuyên đến vậy, đến mức giờ tôi mơ bằng thanh điệu của bà. Một ngày bà nói với tôi, thật chậm rãi, để tôi hiểu, rằng con trai bà đã ra nước ngoài và gần như không có người nước ngoài nào từng buồn học lấy một từ trong ngôn ngữ của bà. Nếu tôi giữ im lặng suốt cả năm đó, tôi sẽ rời thành phố mà nghĩ rằng đó là một nơi lạnh lùng đầy những người lạ bận rộn. Thay vào đó tôi nhận ra rằng sự lạnh lùng đó là của tôi — một bức tường tôi đã dựng lên từ chính nỗi sợ nghe có vẻ ngớ ngẩn của mình. Mười câu nói đó không làm tôi thông thạo. Chúng chưa bao giờ là về sự thông thạo. Chúng là một cách nói, bằng một thứ ngôn ngữ tôi hầu như không biết, rằng bà ấy đáng để mình bỏ công cố gắng. Và hóa ra, đó là toàn bộ thông điệp.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect to show a change completed before a later past point?
options: By the end of the exchange, I have stopped being a stranger.
By the end of the exchange, I had stopped being a stranger.
By the end of the exchange, I had stop being a stranger.
By the end of the exchange, I was stopped being a stranger.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = had + past participle ('stopped'), used for an event completed before another past time. Option 2 uses present perfect 'have', which clashes with the past time frame. Option 3 uses the base verb 'stop'. Option 4 uses 'was stopped', a passive that wrongly suggests someone stopped the narrator.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect = had + past participle; use it for the earlier of two past events.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional correctly to imagine a different past?
options: If I had stayed silent, I would left thinking the city was cold.
If I would have stayed silent, I would have left thinking the city was cold.
If I had stayed silent, I would have left thinking the city was cold.
If I stayed silent, I would have left thinking the city was cold.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional = if + past perfect, would have + past participle. Option 2 drops 'have' from the result. Option 3 wrongly puts 'would have' in the if-clause. Option 4 uses past simple in the if-clause, mixing conditional types.
mcQuestions.hint: Unreal past: if + had + participle, then would have + participle. Keep 'would have' out of the if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede the narrator's mistakes?
options: Although I still made mistakes, but she waited for me to try the words.
Although I still made mistakes she waited for me to try the words.
I still made mistakes, although however she waited for me to try the words.
Although I still made mistakes, she waited for me to try the words.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' is correct. Option 2 adds 'but'. Option 3 omits the comma. Option 4 stacks 'although' and 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' needs a comma and stands alone, without 'but' or 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a repeated past-to-present action?
options: She has corrected my pronunciation so patiently and so often.
She has correct my pronunciation so patiently and so often.
She corrected my pronunciation so patiently and so often, until now.
She has been corrected my pronunciation so patiently and so often.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'has corrected' fits a repeated action continuing to the present. Option 2 uses the base verb 'correct'. Option 3 adds 'until now' to a past simple, which is contradictory. Option 4 uses a passive ('has been corrected'), which wrongly makes the narrator's pronunciation the corrector.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect = has + past participle; the participle of 'correct' is 'corrected'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes the seller saving good produce?
options: She began setting beside the best herbs for me.
She began setting aside the best herbs for me.
She began setting away the best herbs for me.
She began setting apart the best herbs for me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Set aside' is the natural phrasal verb for reserving something. 'Set beside' means to place next to. 'Set away' is not standard. 'Set apart' usually means to distinguish or separate, not to reserve.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrasal verb means 'to keep something back for later'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'made + person + base verb' correctly?
options: She made me to say it again.
She made me saying it again.
She made me say it again.
She made that I say it again.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make someone do something' takes the bare infinitive ('say'). Option 2 adds 'to'. Option 3 uses '-ing'. Option 4 uses an unnatural 'that' clause after 'make'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Make someone' is followed by the base verb, with no 'to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'worth + the effort of + gerund'?
options: I wanted to show that she was worth the effort of to try.
I wanted to show that she was worth the effort of try.
I wanted to show that she was worth to the effort of trying.
I wanted to show that she was worth the effort of trying.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Worth the effort of + gerund' is correct. Option 2 uses 'to try' after 'of'. Option 3 uses the base verb 'try'. Option 4 inserts a stray 'to' after 'worth'.
mcQuestions.hint: After the preposition 'of', use the '-ing' form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes overcoming the difficulty of starting?
options: Those first words helped me break the ice.
Those first words helped me break the cold.
Those first words helped me crack the ice.
Those first words helped me break some ice apart.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Break the ice' is the fixed idiom for easing initial awkwardness. 'Break the cold' is not an idiom. 'Crack the ice' is literal, not idiomatic here. 'Break some ice apart' is literal and changes the meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: There is a set idiom about starting a friendly interaction; it uses 'ice'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly contrasts a transaction with a relationship using 'no longer'?
options: I was no longer just a customer, I was someone she recognise.
I was no longer just a customer; I was someone she recognised.
I was no more longer just a customer; I was someone she recognised.
I was not longer just a customer; I was someone she recognised.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No longer' before the verb phrase is correct, with 'recognised' agreeing in the past. Option 2 uses 'recognise' (present) after a past subject and a comma splice. Option 3 says 'no more longer', which is redundant. Option 4 says 'not longer', which is ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'no longer' (not 'not longer' or 'no more longer') and keep the verb in the past.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best states the story's closing idea clearly and naturally?
options: The phrases were never about fluency, they were a way of say she was worth the effort.
The phrases were never about fluency; they were a way of said she was worth the effort.
The phrases were never about fluency; they were a way of saying she was worth the effort.
The phrases were never about a fluency; they were a way to saying she was worth the effort.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option 1 uses the gerund 'saying' after 'way of' and a semicolon between clauses. Option 2 uses 'say' and a comma splice. Option 3 uses 'said' after 'way of'. Option 4 wrongly adds an article ('a fluency') and uses 'to saying'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'a way of saying'; the noun 'fluency' takes no article here.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator realises the coldness he blamed on the city was really his own fear of sounding foolish. Have you ever avoided trying something — a language, a conversation, a new place — because you were afraid of looking silly? What did that fear cost you, and what might a small attempt have changed?
